title: Expostulation.
content:
آ آ آ آ Tantane, tam patiens, nullo certamine tolli
آ آ آ آ Dona sines?
آ آ آ آ - Virg.



آ آ آ آ Why, weeps the muse for England? What appears
آ آ آ آ In Englandï؟½s case to move the muse to tears?
آ آ آ آ From side to side of her delightful isle
آ آ آ آ Is she not clothed with a perpetual smile?
آ آ آ آ Can Nature add a charm, or Art confer
آ آ آ آ A new-found luxury, not seen in her?
آ آ آ آ Where under heaven is pleasure more pursued,
آ آ آ آ Or where does cold reflection less intrude?
آ آ آ آ Her fields a rich expanse of wavy corn,
آ آ آ آ Pourï؟½d out from Plentyï؟½s overflowing horn;
آ آ آ آ Ambrosial gardens, in which art supplies
آ آ آ آ The fervour and the force of Indian skies;
آ آ آ آ Her peaceful shores, where busy Commerce waits
آ آ آ آ To pour his golden tide through all her gates;
آ آ آ آ Whom fiery suns, that scorch the russet spice
آ آ آ آ Of eastern groves, and oceans floorï؟½d with ice,
آ آ آ آ Forbid in vain to push his daring way
آ آ آ آ To darker climes, or climes of brighter day;
آ آ آ آ Whom the winds waft whereï؟½er the billows roll,
آ آ آ آ From the Worldï؟½s girdle to the frozen pole;
آ آ آ آ The chariots bounding in her wheel-worn streets,
آ آ آ آ Her vaults below, where every vintage meets;
آ آ آ آ Her theatres, her revels, and her sports;
آ آ آ آ The scenes to which not youth alone resorts,
آ آ آ آ But age, in spite of weakness and of pain,
آ آ آ آ Still haunts, in hope to dream of youth again;
آ آ آ آ All speak her happy: let the muse look round
آ آ آ آ From East to West, no sorrow can be found;
آ آ آ آ Or only what, in cottages confined,
آ آ آ آ Sighs unregarded to the passing wind.
آ آ آ آ Then wherefore weep for England? What appears
آ آ آ آ In Englandï؟½s case to move the muse to tears?
آ آ آ آ The prophet wept for Israel; wishï؟½d his eyes
آ آ آ آ Were fountains fed with infinite supplies;
آ آ آ آ For Israel dealt in robbery and wrong;
آ آ آ آ There were the scornerï؟½s and the slandererï؟½s tongue;
آ آ آ آ Oaths, used as playthings or convenient tools,
آ آ آ آ As interest biassï؟½d knaves, or fashion fools;
آ آ آ آ Adultery, neighing at his neighbourï؟½s door;
آ آ آ آ Oppression labouring hard to grind the poor;
آ آ آ آ The partial balance and deceitful weight;
آ آ آ آ The treacherous smile, a mask for secret hate;
آ آ آ آ Hypocrisy, formality in prayer,
آ آ آ آ And the dull service of the lip were there.
آ آ آ آ Her women, insolent and self-carressï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ By Vanityï؟½s unwearied finger dressï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Forgot the blush that virgin fears impart
آ آ آ آ To modest cheeks, and borrowï؟½d one from art;
آ آ آ آ Were just such trifles, without worth or use,
آ آ آ آ As silly pride and idleness produce;
آ آ آ آ Curlï؟½d, scented, furbelowï؟½d, and flounced around,
آ آ آ آ With feet too delicate to touch the ground,
آ آ آ آ They stretchï؟½d the neck, and rollï؟½d the wanton eye,
آ آ آ آ And sighï؟½d for every fool that flutterï؟½d by.
آ آ آ آ He saw his people slaves to every lust,
آ آ آ آ Lewd, avaricious, arrogant, unjust;
آ آ آ آ He heard the wheels of an avenging God
آ آ آ آ Groan heavily along the distant road;
آ آ آ آ Saw Babylon set wide her two-leaved brass
آ آ آ آ To let the military deluge pass;
آ آ آ آ Jerusalem a prey, her glory soilï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Her princes captive, and her treasures spoilï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Wept till all Israel heard his bitter cry,
آ آ آ آ Stampï؟½d with his foot, and smote upon his thigh;
آ آ آ آ But wept, and stampï؟½d, and smote his thigh in vain,
آ آ آ آ Pleasure is deaf when told of future pain,
آ آ آ آ And sounds prophetic are too rough to suit
آ آ آ آ Ears long accustomï؟½d to the pleasing lute:
آ آ آ آ They scornï؟½d his inspiration and his theme,
آ آ آ آ Pronouncï؟½d him frantic, and his fears a dream;
آ آ آ آ With self-indulgence wingï؟½d the fleeting hours,
آ آ آ آ Till the foe found them, and down fell the towers.
آ آ آ آ Long time Assyria bound them in her chain,
آ آ آ آ Till penitence had purged the public stain,
آ آ آ آ And Cyrus, with relenting pity moved,
آ آ آ آ Returnï؟½d them happy to the land they loved;
آ آ آ آ There, proof against prosperity, a while
آ آ آ آ They stood the test of her ensnaring smile,
آ آ آ آ And had the grace in scenes of peace to shew
آ آ آ آ The virtue they had learnï؟½d in scenes of woe.
آ آ آ آ But man is frail, and can but ill sustain
آ آ آ آ A long immunity from grief and pain;
آ آ آ آ And, after all the joys that Plenty leads,
آ آ آ آ With tiptoe step Vice silently succeeds.
آ آ آ آ When he that ruled them with a shepherdï؟½s rod,
آ آ آ آ In form a man, in dignity a God,
آ آ آ آ Came, not expected in that humble guise,
آ آ آ آ To sift and search them with unerring eyes,
آ آ آ آ He found concealï؟½d beneath a fair outside,
آ آ آ آ The filth of rottenness, and worm of pride;
آ آ آ آ Their piety a system of deceit,
آ آ آ آ Scripture employï؟½d to sanctify the cheat;
آ آ آ آ The Pharisee the dupe of his own art,
آ آ آ آ Self-idolised, and yet a knave at heart.
آ آ آ آ When nations are to perish in their sins,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis in the church the leprosy begins;
آ آ آ آ The priest, whose office is, with zeal sincere,
آ آ آ آ To watch the fountain and preserve it clear,
آ آ آ آ Carelessly nods and sleeps upon the brink,
آ آ آ آ While others poison what the flock must drink;
آ آ آ آ Or, waking at the call of lust alone,
آ آ آ آ Infuses lies and errors of his own:
آ آ آ آ His unsuspecting sheep believe it pure;
آ آ آ آ And, tainted by the very means of cure,
آ آ آ آ Catch from each other a contagious spot,
آ آ آ آ The foul forerunner of a general rot.
آ آ آ آ Then truth is hushï؟½d, that Heresy may preach;
آ آ آ آ And all is trash that reason cannot reach;
آ آ آ آ Then Godï؟½s own image on the soul impressï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Becomes a mockery and a standing jest;
آ آ آ آ And faith, the root whence only can arise
آ آ آ آ The graces of a life that wins the skies,
آ آ آ آ Loses at once all value and esteem,
آ آ آ آ Pronounced by graybeards a pernicious dream:
آ آ آ آ Then Ceremony leads her bigots forth,
آ آ آ آ Prepared to fight for shadows of no worth;
آ آ آ آ While truths, on which eternal things depend,
آ آ آ آ Find not, or hardly find, a single friend:
آ آ آ آ As soldiers watch the signal of command,
آ آ آ آ They learn to bow, to kneel, to sit, to stand;
آ آ آ آ Happy to fill religionï؟½s vacant place,
آ آ آ آ With hollow form, and gesture, and grimace.
آ آ آ آ Such, when the Teacher of his church was there,
آ آ آ آ People and priest, the sons of Israel were;
آ آ آ آ Stiff in the letter, lax in the design
آ آ آ آ And import of their oracles divine;
آ آ آ آ Their learning legendary, false, absurd,
آ آ آ آ And yet exalted above Godï؟½s own word;
آ آ آ آ They drew a curse from an intended good,
آ آ آ آ Puffï؟½d up with gifts they never understood.
آ آ آ آ He judged them with as terrible a frown,
آ آ آ آ As if not love, but wrath, had brought him down:
آ آ آ آ Yet he was gentle as soft summer airs,
آ آ آ آ Had grace for othersï؟½ sins, but none for theirs;
آ آ آ آ Through all he spoke a noble plainnness ranï؟½
آ آ آ آ Rhetoric is artifice, the work of man;
آ آ آ آ And tricks and turns, that fancy may devise,
آ آ آ آ Are far too mean for Him that rules the skies.
آ آ آ آ The astonishï؟½d vulgar trembled while he tore
آ آ آ آ The mask from faces never seen before;
آ آ آ آ He strippï؟½d the impostors in the noonday sun,
آ آ آ آ Shewï؟½d that they followï؟½d all they seemï؟½d to shun;
آ آ آ آ Their prayers made public, their excesses kept
آ آ آ آ As private as the chambers where they slept;
آ آ آ آ The temple and its holy rites profaned
آ آ آ آ By mummeries He that dwelt in it disdainï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Uplifted hands, that at convenient times
آ آ آ آ Could act extortion and the worst of crimes,
آ آ آ آ Washï؟½d with a neatness scrupulously nice,
آ آ آ آ And free from every taint but that of vice.
آ آ آ آ Judgment, however tardy, mends her pace
آ آ آ آ When obstinacy once has conquerï؟½d grace.
آ آ آ آ They saw distemper healï؟½d, and life restored,
آ آ آ آ In answer to the fiat of his word;
آ آ آ آ Confessï؟½d the wonder, and with daring tongue
آ آ آ آ Blasphemed the authority from which it sprung.
آ آ آ آ They knew, by sure prognostics seen on high,
آ آ آ آ The future tone and temper of the sky;
آ آ آ آ But, grave dissemblers! could not understand
آ آ آ آ That sin let loose speaks punishment at hand.
آ آ آ آ Ask now of historyï؟½s authentic page,
آ آ آ آ And call up evidence from every age;
آ آ آ آ Display with busy and laborious hand
آ آ آ آ The blessings of the most indebted land;
آ آ آ آ What nation will you find, whose annals prove
آ آ آ آ So rich an interest in Almighty love?
آ آ آ آ Where dwell they now, where dwelt in ancient day
آ آ آ آ A people planted, waterï؟½d, blest, as they?
آ آ آ آ Let Egyptï؟½s plagues and Canaanï؟½s woes proclaim
آ آ آ آ The favours pourï؟½d upon the Jewish name;
آ آ آ آ Their freedom purchased for them at the cost
آ آ آ آ Of all their hard oppressors valued most:
آ آ آ آ Their title to a country not their own
آ آ آ آ Made sure by prodigies till then unknown;
آ آ آ آ For them the states they left made waste and void;
آ آ آ آ For them the states to which they went destroyï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ A cloud to measure out their march by day,
آ آ آ آ By night a fire to cheer the gloomy way;
آ آ آ آ That moving signal summoning, when best,
آ آ آ آ Their host to move, and, when it stayï؟½d, to rest.
آ آ آ آ For them the rocks dissolved into a flood,
آ آ آ آ The dews condensed into angelic food,
آ آ آ آ Their very garments sacred, old yet new,
آ آ آ آ And Time forbid to touch them as he flew;
آ آ آ آ Streams, swellï؟½d above the bank, enjoinï؟½d to stand
آ آ آ آ While they passï؟½d through to their appointed land;
آ آ آ آ Their leader armï؟½d with meekness, zeal, and love,
آ آ آ آ And graced with clear credentials from above;
آ آ آ آ Themselves secured beneath the Almighty wing;
آ آ آ آ Their God their captain,[1] lawgiver, and king;
آ آ آ آ Crownï؟½d with a thousand victories, and at last
آ آ آ آ Lords of the conquerï؟½d soil, there rooted fast,
آ آ آ آ In peace possessing what they won by war,
آ آ آ آ Their name far publishï؟½d, and revered as far;
آ آ آ آ Where will you find a race like theirs, endowï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With all that man eï؟½er wishï؟½d, or heaven bestowï؟½d?
آ آ آ آ They, and they only, amongst all mankind,
آ آ آ آ Received the transcript of the Eternal Mind;
آ آ آ آ Were trusted with his own engraven laws,
آ آ آ آ And constituted guardians of his cause;
آ آ آ آ Theirs were the prophets, theirs the priestly call,
آ آ آ آ And theirs by birth the Saviour of us all.
آ آ آ آ In vain the nations, that had seen them rise
آ آ آ آ With fierce and envious, yet admiring, eyes,
آ آ آ آ Had sought to crush them, guarded as they were
آ آ آ آ By power divine and skill that could not err.
آ آ آ آ Had they maintainï؟½d allegiance firm and sure,
آ آ آ آ And kept the faith immaculate and pure,
آ آ آ آ Then the proud eagles of all-conquering Rome
آ آ آ آ Had found one city not to be oï؟½ercome;
آ آ آ آ And the twelve standards of the tribes unfurlï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Had bid defiance to the warring world.
آ آ آ آ But grace abused brings forth the foulest deeds,
آ آ آ آ As richest soil the most luxuriant weeds.
آ آ آ آ Cured of the golden calves, their fathersï؟½ sin,
آ آ آ آ They set up self, that idol god within;
آ آ آ آ Viewï؟½d a Deliverer with disdain and hate,
آ آ آ آ Who left them still a tributary state;
آ آ آ آ Seized fast his hand, held out to set them free
آ آ آ آ From a worse yoke, and nailï؟½d it to the tree:
آ آ آ آ There was the consummation and the crown,
آ آ آ آ The flower of Israelï؟½s infamy full blown;
آ آ آ آ Thence date their sad declension, and their fall,
آ آ آ آ Their woes, not yet repealï؟½d, thence date them all.
آ آ آ آ Thus fell the best instructed in her day,
آ آ آ آ And the most favourï؟½d land, look where we may.
آ آ آ آ Philosophy indeed on Grecian eyes
آ آ آ آ Had pourï؟½d the day, and clearï؟½d the Roman skies;
آ آ آ آ In other climes perhaps creative art,
آ آ آ آ With power surpassing theirs, performï؟½d her part;
آ آ آ آ Might give more life to marble, or might fill
آ آ آ آ The glowing tablets with a juster skill,
آ آ آ آ Might shine in fable, and grace idle themes
آ آ آ آ With all the embroidery of poetic dreams;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas theirs alone to dive into the plan
آ آ آ آ That truth and mercy had revealï؟½d to man;
آ آ آ آ And, while the world beside, that plan unknown,
آ آ آ آ Deified useless wood or senseless stone,
آ آ آ آ They breathed in faith their well-directed prayers,
آ آ آ آ And the true God, the God of truth, was theirs.
آ آ آ آ Their glory faded, and their race dispersed,
آ آ آ آ The last of nations now, though once the first,
آ آ آ آ They warn and teach the proudest, would they learn,
آ آ آ آ Keep wisdom, or meet vengeance in your turn:
آ آ آ آ If we escaped not, if Heaven spared not us,
آ آ آ آ Peelï؟½d, scatterï؟½d, and exterminated thus;
آ آ آ آ If vice received her retribution due,
آ آ آ آ When we were visited, what hope for you?
آ آ آ آ When God arises with an awful frown,
آ آ آ آ To punish lust, or pluck presumption down;
آ آ آ آ When gifts perverted, or not duly prized,
آ آ آ آ Pleasure oï؟½ervalued, and his grace despised,
آ آ آ آ Provoke the vengeance of his righteous hand,
آ آ آ آ To pour down wrath upon a thankless land:
آ آ آ آ He will be found impartially severe,
آ آ آ آ Too just to wink, or speak the guilty clear.
آ آ آ آ Oh Israel, of all nations most undone!
آ آ آ آ Thy diadem displaced, thy sceptre gone;
آ آ آ آ Thy temple, once thy glory, fallen and razed,
آ آ آ آ And thou a worshipper eï؟½en where thou mayst,
آ آ آ آ They services, once holy without spot,
آ آ آ آ Mere shadows now, their ancient pomp forgot;
آ آ آ آ Thy Levites, once a consecrated host,
آ آ آ آ No longer Levites, and their lineage lost,
آ آ آ آ And thou thyself oï؟½er every country sown,
آ آ آ آ With none on earth that thou canst call thine own;
آ آ آ آ Cry aloud, thou that sittest in the dust,
آ آ آ آ Cry to the proud, the cruel, and unjust;
آ آ آ آ Knock at the gates of nations, rouse their fears;
آ آ آ آ Say wrath is coming, and the storm appears;
آ آ آ آ But raise the shrillest cry in British ears.
آ آ آ آ What ails thee, restless as the waves that roar,
آ آ آ آ And fling their foam against thy chalky shore?
آ آ آ آ Mistress, at least while Providence shall please,
آ آ آ آ And trident-bearing queen of the wide seasï؟½
آ آ آ آ Why, having kept good faith, and often shewn
آ آ آ آ Friendship and truth to others, findï؟½st thou none?
آ آ آ آ Thou that hast set the persecuted free,
آ آ آ آ None interposes now to succour thee.
آ آ آ آ Countries indebted to thy power, that shine
آ آ آ آ With light derived from thee, would smother thine.
آ آ آ آ Thy very children watch for thy disgrace,
آ آ آ آ A lawless brood, and curse thee to thy face.
آ آ آ آ Thy rulers load thy credit, year by year,
آ آ آ آ With sums Peruvian mines could never clear;
آ آ آ آ As if, like arches built with skilful hand,
آ آ آ آ The more ï؟½twere pressï؟½d the firmer it would stand.
آ آ آ آ The cry in all thy ships is still the same,
آ آ آ آ Speed us away to battle and to fame.
آ آ آ آ Thy mariners explore the wild expanse,
آ آ آ آ Impatient to descry the flags of France:
آ آ آ آ But, though they fight as thine have ever fought,
آ آ آ آ Return ashamed without the wreaths they sought.
آ آ آ آ Thy senate is a scene of civil jar,
آ آ آ آ Chaos of contrarieties at war;
آ آ آ آ Where sharp and solid, phlegmatic and light,
آ آ آ آ Discordant atoms meet, ferment, and fight;
آ آ آ آ Where obstinacy takes his sturdy stand,
آ آ آ آ To disconcert what policy has plannï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Where policy is busied all night long
آ آ آ آ In setting right what faction has set wrong;
آ آ آ آ Where flails of oratory thresh the floor,
آ آ آ آ That yields them chaff and dust, and nothing more.
آ آ آ آ Thy rackï؟½d inhabitants repine, complain,
آ آ آ آ Taxï؟½d till the brow of labour sweats in vain;
آ آ آ آ War lays a burden on the reeling state,
آ آ آ آ And peace does nothing to relieve the weight;
آ آ آ آ Successive loads succeeding broils impose,
آ آ آ آ And sighing millions prophesy the close.
آ آ آ آ Is adverse Providence, when ponderï؟½d well,
آ آ آ آ So dimly writ, or difficult to spell,
آ آ آ آ Thou canst not read with readiness and ease
آ آ آ آ Providence adverse in events like these?
آ آ آ آ Know then that heavenly wisdom on this ball
آ آ آ آ Creates, gives birth to, guides, consummates all;
آ آ آ آ That, while laborious and quick-thoughted man
آ آ آ آ Snuffs up the praise of what he seems to plan,
آ آ آ آ He first conceives, then perfects his design,
آ آ آ آ As a mere instrument in hands divine:
آ آ آ آ Blind to the working of that sacred power
آ آ آ آ That balances the wings of every hour,
آ آ آ آ The busy trifler dreams himself alone,
آ آ آ آ Frames many a purpose, and God works his own.
آ آ آ آ States thrive or wither as moons wax and wane,
آ آ آ آ Eï؟½en as his will and his decrees ordain;
آ آ آ آ While honour, virtue, piety, bear sway,
آ آ آ آ They flourish; and, as these decline, decay:
آ آ آ آ In just resentment of his injured laws,
آ آ آ آ He pours contempt on them and on their cause;
آ آ آ آ Strikes the rough thread of error right athwart
آ آ آ آ The web of every scheme they have at heart;
آ آ آ آ Bids rottenness invade and bring to dust
آ آ آ آ The pillars of support, in which they trust,
آ آ آ آ And do his errand of disgrace and shame
آ آ آ آ On the chief strength and glory of the frame.
آ آ آ آ None ever yet impeded what he wrought,
آ آ آ آ None bars him out from his most secret thought;
آ آ آ آ Darkness itself before his eye is light,
آ آ آ آ And hellï؟½s close mischief naked in his sight.
آ آ آ آ Stand now and judge thyselfï؟½Hast thou incurrï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His anger who can waste thee with a word,
آ آ آ آ Who poises and proportions sea and land,
آ آ آ آ Weighing them in the hollow of his hand,
آ آ آ آ And in whose awful sight all nations seem
آ آ آ آ As grasshoppers, as dust, a drop, a dream?
آ آ آ آ Hast thou (a sacrilege his soul abhors)
آ آ آ آ Claimï؟½d all the glory of thy prosperous wars?
آ آ آ آ Proud of thy fleets and armies, stolen the gem
آ آ آ آ Of his just praise, to lavish it on them?
آ آ آ آ Hast thou not learnï؟½d, what thou art often told,
آ آ آ آ A truth still sacred, and believed of old,
آ آ آ آ That no success attends on spears and swords
آ آ آ آ Unblest, and that the battle is the Lordï؟½s?
آ آ آ آ That courage is his creature; and dismay
آ آ آ آ The post, that at his bidding speeds away,
آ آ آ آ Ghastly in feature, and his stammering tongue
آ آ آ آ With doleful humour and sad presage hung,
آ آ آ آ To quell the valour of the stoutest heart,
آ آ آ آ And teach the combatant a womanï؟½s part?
آ آ آ آ That he bids thousands fly when none pursue,
آ آ آ آ Saves as he will by many or by few,
آ آ آ آ And claims for ever, as his royal right,
آ آ آ آ The event and sure decision of the fight?
آ آ آ آ Hast thou, though suckled at fair freedomï؟½s breast,
آ آ آ آ Exported slavery to the conquerï؟½d East?
آ آ آ آ Pullï؟½d down the tyrants India served with dread,
آ آ آ آ And raised thyself, a greater, in their stead?
آ آ آ آ Gone thither, armï؟½d and hungry, returnï؟½d full,
آ آ آ آ Fed from the richest veins of the Mogul,
آ آ آ آ A despot big with power obtainï؟½d by wealth,
آ آ آ آ And that obtainï؟½d by rapine and by stealth?
آ آ آ آ With Asiatic vices stored thy mind,
آ آ آ آ But left their virtues and thine own behind?
آ آ آ آ And, having truckï؟½d thy soul, brought home the fee,
آ آ آ آ To tempt the poor to sell himself to thee?
آ آ آ آ Hast thou by statue shoved from its design,
آ آ آ آ The Saviourï؟½s feast, his own blest bread and wine,
آ آ آ آ And made the symbols of atoning grace
آ آ آ آ An office-key, a picklock to a place,
آ آ آ آ That infidels may prove their title good
آ آ آ آ By an oath dippï؟½d in sacramental blood?
آ آ آ آ A blot that will be still a blot, in spite
آ آ آ آ Of all that grave apologists may write;
آ آ آ آ And though a bishop toilï؟½d to cleanse the stain,
آ آ آ آ He wipes and scours the silver cup in vain.
آ آ آ آ And hast thou sworn, on every slight pretence,
آ آ آ آ Till perjuries are common as bad pence,
آ آ آ آ While thousands, careless of the damning sin,
آ آ آ آ Kiss the bookï؟½s outside, who neï؟½er look within?
آ آ آ آ Hast thou, when Heaven has clothed thee with disgrace,
آ آ آ آ And, long provoked, repaid thee to thy face
آ آ آ آ (For thou hast known eclipses, and endured
آ آ آ آ Dimness and anguish, all thy beams obscured,
آ آ آ آ When sin has shed dishonour on thy brow;
آ آ آ آ And never of a sabler hue than now),
آ آ آ آ Hast thou, with heart perverse and conscience searï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Despising all rebuke, still persevered,
آ آ آ آ And, having chosen evil, scornï؟½d the voice
آ آ آ آ That cried, Repent!ï؟½and gloried in thy choice?
آ آ آ آ Thy fastings, when calamity at last
آ آ آ آ Suggests the expedient of a yearly fast,
آ آ آ آ What mean they? Canst thou dream there is a power
آ آ آ آ In lighter diet at a later hour,
آ آ آ آ To charm to sleep the threatening of the skies,
آ آ آ آ And hide past folly from all-seeing eyes?
آ آ آ آ The fast that wins deliverance, and suspends
آ آ آ آ The stroke that a vindictive God intends,
آ آ آ آ Is to renounce hypocrisy; to draw
آ آ آ آ Thy life upon the pattern of the law;
آ آ آ آ To war with pleasure, idolized before;
آ آ آ آ To vanquish lust, and wear its yoke no more.
آ آ آ آ All fasting else, whateï؟½er be the pretence,
آ آ آ آ Is wooing mercy by renewï؟½d offence.
آ آ آ آ Hast thou within thee sin, that in old time
آ آ آ آ Brought fire from heaven, the sex-abusing crime,
آ آ آ آ Whose horrid perpetration stamps disgrace,
آ آ آ آ Baboons are free from, upon human race?
آ آ آ آ Think on the fruitful and well-waterï؟½d spot
آ آ آ آ That fed the flocks and herds of wealthy Lot,
آ آ آ آ Where Paradise seemï؟½d still vouchsafed on earth,
آ آ آ آ Burning and scorchï؟½d into perpetual dearth,
آ آ آ آ Or, in his words who damnï؟½d the base desire,
آ آ آ آ Suffering the vengeance of eternal fire:
آ آ آ آ Then nature, injured, scandalized, defiled,
آ آ آ آ Unveilï؟½d her blushing cheek, lookï؟½d on, and smiled;
آ آ آ آ Beheld with joy the lovely scene defaced,
آ آ آ آ And praised the wrath that laid her beauties waste.
آ آ آ آ Far be the thought from any verse of mine,
آ آ آ آ And farther still the formï؟½d and fixï؟½d design,
آ آ آ آ To thrust the charge of deeds that I detest
آ آ آ آ Against an innocent unconscious breast;
آ آ آ آ The man that dares traduce, because he can
آ آ آ آ With safety to himself, is not a man:
آ آ آ آ An individual is a sacred mark,
آ آ آ آ Not to be pierced in play, or in the dark;
آ آ آ آ But public censure speaks a public foe,
آ آ آ آ Unless a zeal for virtue guide the blow.
آ آ آ آ The priestly brotherhood, devout, sincere,
آ آ آ آ From mean self-interest and ambition clear,
آ آ آ آ Their hope in heaven, servility their scorn,
آ آ آ آ Prompt to persuade, expostulate, and warn,
آ آ آ آ Their wisdom pure, and given them from above,
آ آ آ آ Their usefulness ensured by zeal and love,
آ آ آ آ As meek as the man Moses, and withal
آ آ آ آ As bold as in Agrippaï؟½s presence Paul,
آ آ آ آ Should fly the worldï؟½s contaminating touch,
آ آ آ آ Holy and unpolluted:ï؟½are thine such?
آ آ آ آ Except a few with Eliï؟½s spirit blest,
آ آ آ آ Hophni and Phineas may describe the rest.
آ آ آ آ Where shall a teacher look, in days like these,
آ آ آ آ For ears and hearts that he can hope to please?
آ آ آ آ Look to the poorï؟½the simple and the plain
آ آ آ آ Will hear perhaps thy salutary strain:
آ آ آ آ Humility is gentle, apt to learn,
آ آ آ آ Speak but the word, will listen and return.
آ آ آ آ Alas! not so; the poorest of the flock
آ آ آ آ Are proud, and set their faces as a rock;
آ آ آ آ Denied that earthly opulence they choose,
آ آ آ آ Godï؟½s better gift they scoff at and refuse.
آ آ آ آ The rich, the produce of a nobler stem,
آ آ آ آ Are more intelligent, at leastï؟½try them.
آ آ آ آ Oh vain inquiry! they without remorse
آ آ آ آ Are altogether gone a devious course;
آ آ آ آ Where beckoning pleasure leads them, wildly stray;
آ آ آ آ Have burst the bands, and cast the yoke away.
آ آ آ آ Now borne upon the wings of truth sublime,
آ آ آ آ Review thy dim original and prime.
آ آ آ آ This island, spot of unreclaimï؟½d rude earth,
آ آ آ آ The cradle that received thee at thy birth,
آ آ آ آ Was rockï؟½d by many a rough Norwegian blast,
آ آ آ آ And Danish howlings scared thee as they passï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ For thou wast born amid the din of arms,
آ آ آ آ And suckï؟½d a breast that panted with alarms.
آ آ آ آ While yet thou wast a grovelling, puling chit,
آ آ آ آ Thy bones not fashionï؟½d, and thy joints not knit,
آ آ آ آ The Roman taught thy stubborn knee to bow,
آ آ آ آ Though twice a Caesar could not bend thee now.
آ آ آ آ His victory was that of orient light,
آ آ آ آ When the sunï؟½s shafts disperse the gloom of night.
آ آ آ آ Thy language at this distant moment shews
آ آ آ آ How much the country to the conqueror owes;
آ آ آ آ Expressive, energetic, and refined,
آ آ آ آ It sparkles with the gems he left behind;
آ آ آ آ He brought thy land a blessing when he came,
آ آ آ آ He found thee savage, and he left thee tame;
آ آ آ آ Taught thee to clothe thy pinkï؟½d and painted hide,
آ آ آ آ And gracï؟½d thy figure with a soldierï؟½s pride;
آ آ آ آ He sowï؟½d the seeds of order where he went,
آ آ آ آ Improved thee far beyond his own intent,
آ آ آ آ And, while he ruled thee by the sword alone,
آ آ آ آ Made thee at last a warrior like his own.
آ آ آ آ Religion, if in heavenly truths attired,
آ آ آ آ Needs only to be seen to be admired;
آ آ آ آ But thine, as dark as witcheries of the night,
آ آ آ آ Was formï؟½d to harden hearts and shock the sight;
آ آ آ آ Thy druids struck the well-hung harps they bore
آ آ آ آ With fingers deeply dyed in human gore;
آ آ آ آ And while the victim slowly bled to death,
آ آ آ آ Upon the rolling chords rung out his dying breath.
آ آ آ آ Who brought the lamp that with awaking beams
آ آ آ آ Dispellï؟½d thy gloom, and broke away thy dreams,
آ آ آ آ Tradition now decrepit and worn out,
آ آ آ آ Babbler of ancient fables, leaves a doubt:
آ آ آ آ But still light reachï؟½d thee: and those gods of thine,
آ آ آ آ Woden and Thor, each tottering in his shrine,
آ آ آ آ Fell broken and defaced at their own door,
آ آ آ آ As Dagon in Philistia long before.
آ آ آ آ But Rome with sorceries and magic wand
آ آ آ آ Soon raised a cloud that darkenï؟½d every land;
آ آ آ آ And thine was smotherï؟½d in the stench and fog
آ آ آ آ Of Tiberï؟½s marshes and the papal bog.
آ آ آ آ Then priests with bulls and briefs, and shaven crowns,
آ آ آ آ And griping fists and unrelenting frowns,
آ آ آ آ Legates and delegates with powers from hell,
آ آ آ آ Though heavenly in pretension, fleeced thee well;
آ آ آ آ And to this hour, to keep it fresh in mind,
آ آ آ آ Some twigs of that old scourge are left behind.[2]
آ آ آ آ Thy soldiery, the Popeï؟½s well-managed pack,
آ آ آ آ Were trainï؟½d beneath his lash, and knew the smack,
آ آ آ آ And, when he laid them on the scent of blood,
آ آ آ آ Would hunt a Saracen through fire and flood.
آ آ آ آ Lavish of life, to win an empty tomb,
آ آ آ آ That proved a mint of wealth, a mine to Rome.
آ آ آ آ They left their bones beneath unfriendly skies,
آ آ آ آ His worthless absolution all the prize.
آ آ آ آ Thou wast the veriest slave, in days of yore,
آ آ آ آ That ever draggï؟½d a chain or tuggï؟½d an oar;
آ آ آ آ Thy monarchs arbitrary, fierce, unjust,
آ آ آ آ Themselves the slaves of bigotry or lust,
آ آ آ آ Disdainï؟½d thy counsels, only in distress
آ آ آ آ Found thee a goodly sponge for power to press.
آ آ آ آ Thy chiefs, the lords of many a petty fee,
آ آ آ آ Provoked and harassï؟½d, in return plagued thee;
آ آ آ آ Callï؟½d thee away from peaceable employ,
آ آ آ آ Domestic happiness and rural joy,
آ آ آ آ To waste thy life in arms, or lay it down
آ آ آ آ In causeless feuds and bickerings of their own.
آ آ آ آ Thy parliaments adored, on bended knees,
آ آ آ آ The sovereignty they were convened to please;
آ آ آ آ Whateï؟½er was askï؟½d, too timid to resist,
آ آ آ آ Complied with, and were graciously dismissï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ And if some Spartan soul a doubt expressï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And, blushing at the tameness of the rest,
آ آ آ آ Dared to suppose the subject had a choice,
آ آ آ آ He was a traitor by the general voice.
آ آ آ آ Oh, slave! with powers thou didst not dare exert,
آ آ آ آ Verse cannot stoop so low as thy desert;
آ آ آ آ It shakes the sides of splenetic disdain,
آ آ آ آ Thou self-entitled ruler of the main,
آ آ آ آ To trace thee to the date, when yon fair sea,
آ آ آ آ That clips thy shores, had no such charms for thee;
آ آ آ آ When other nations flew from coast to coast,
آ آ آ آ And thou hadst neither fleet nor flag to boast.
آ آ آ آ Kneel now, and lay thy forehead in the dust;
آ آ آ آ Blush if thou canst; not petrified, thou must;
آ آ آ آ Act but an honest and a faithful part;
آ آ آ آ Compare what then thou wast with what thou art;
آ آ آ آ And Godï؟½s disposing providence confessï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Obduracy itself must yield the rest.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then thou art bound to serve him, and to prove,
آ آ آ آ Hour after hour, thy gratitude and love.
آ آ آ آ Has he not hid thee and thy favourï؟½d land,
آ آ آ آ For ages, safe beneath his sheltering hand,
آ آ آ آ Given thee his blessing on the clearest proof,
آ آ آ آ Bid nations leagued against thee stand aloof,
آ آ آ آ And charged hostility and hate to roar
آ آ آ آ Where else they would, but not upon thy shore?
آ آ آ آ His power secured thee when presumptuous Spain
آ آ آ آ Baptized her fleet invincible in vain;
آ آ آ آ Her gloomy monarch, doubtful and resignï؟½d
آ آ آ آ To every pang that racks an anxious mind,
آ آ آ آ Askï؟½d of the waves that broke upon his coast,
آ آ آ آ What tidings? and the surge repliedï؟½All lost!
آ آ آ آ And when the Stuart, leaning on the Scot,
آ آ آ آ Then too much fearï؟½d, and now too much forgot,
آ آ آ آ Pierced to the very centre of the realm,
آ آ آ آ And hoped to seize his abdicated helm,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas but to prove how quickly, with a frown,
آ آ آ آ He that had raised thee could have pluckï؟½d thee down.
آ آ آ آ Peculiar is the grace by thee possessï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Thy foes implacable, thy land at rest;
آ آ آ آ Thy thunders travel over earth and seas,
آ آ آ آ And all at home is pleasure, wealth, and ease.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis thus, extending his tempestuous arm,
آ آ آ آ Thy Maker fills the nations with alarm,
آ آ آ آ While his own heaven surveys the troubled scene,
آ آ آ آ And feels no change, unshaken and serene.
آ آ آ آ Freedom, in other lands scarce known to shine,
آ آ آ آ Pours out a flood of splendour upon thine;
آ آ آ آ Thou hast as bright an interest in her rays
آ آ آ آ As ever Roman had in Romeï؟½s best days.
آ آ آ آ True freedom is where no restraint is known
آ آ آ آ That Scripture, justice, and good sense disown.
آ آ آ آ Where only vice and injury are tied,
آ آ آ آ And all from shore to shore is free beside.
آ آ آ آ Such freedom isï؟½and Windsorï؟½s hoary towers
آ آ آ آ Stood trembling at the boldness of thy powers,
آ آ آ آ That won a nymph on that immortal plain,
آ آ آ آ Like her the fabled Phoebus wooï؟½d in vain;
آ آ آ آ He found the laurel onlyï؟½happier you
آ آ آ آ The unfading laurel, and the virgin too![3]
آ آ آ آ Now think, if pleasure have a thought to spare;
آ آ آ آ If God himself be not beneath her care;
آ آ آ آ If business, constant as the wheels of time,
آ آ آ آ Can pause an hour to read a serious rhyme;
آ آ آ آ If the new mail thy merchants now receive,
آ آ آ آ Or expectation of the next, give leave;
آ آ آ آ Oh think, if chargeable with deep arrears
آ آ آ آ For such indulgence gilding all thy years,
آ آ آ آ How much, though long neglected, shining yet,
آ آ آ آ The beams of heavenly truth have swellï؟½d the debt.
آ آ آ آ When persecuting zeal made royal sport
آ آ آ آ With tortured innocence in Maryï؟½s court,
آ آ آ آ And Bonner, blithe as shepherd at a wake,
آ آ آ آ Enjoyï؟½d the show, and danced about the stake,
آ آ آ آ The sacred book, its value understood,
آ آ آ آ Received the seal of martyrdom in blood.
آ آ آ آ Those holy men, so full of truth and grace,
آ آ آ آ Seem to reflection of a different race,
آ آ آ آ Meek, modest, venerable, wise, sincere,
آ آ آ آ In such a cause they could not dare to fear;
آ آ آ آ They could not purchase earth with such a prize,
آ آ آ آ Or spare a life too short to reach the skies.
آ آ آ آ From them to thee conveyï؟½d along the tide,
آ آ آ آ Their streaming hearts pourï؟½d freely when they died;
آ آ آ آ Those truths, which neither use nor years impair,
آ آ آ آ Invite thee, woo thee, to the bliss they share.
آ آ آ آ What dotage will not vanity maintain?
آ آ آ آ What web too weak to catch a modern brain?
آ آ آ آ The moles and bats in full assembly find,
آ آ آ آ On special search, the keen-eyed eagle blind.
آ آ آ آ And did they dream, and art thou wiser now?
آ آ آ آ Prove itï؟½if better, I submit and bow.
آ آ آ آ Wisdom and goodness are twin-born, one heart
آ آ آ آ Must hold both sisters, never seen apart.
آ آ آ آ So thenï؟½as darkness overspread the deep,
آ آ آ آ Ere nature rose from her eternal sleep,
آ آ آ آ And this delightful earth, and that fair sky,
آ آ آ آ Leapï؟½d out of nothing, callï؟½d by the Most High;
آ آ آ آ By such a change thy darkness is made light,
آ آ آ آ Thy chaos order, and thy weakness might;
آ آ آ آ And He, whose power mere nullity obeys,
آ آ آ آ Who found thee nothing, formï؟½d thee for his praise.
آ آ آ آ To praise him is to serve him , and fulfil,
آ آ آ آ Doing and suffering, his unquestionï؟½d will;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis to believe what men inspired of old,
آ آ آ آ Faithful, and faithfully informï؟½d, unfold;
آ آ آ آ Candid and just, with no false aim in view,
آ آ آ آ To take for truth what cannot but be true;
آ آ آ آ To learn in Godï؟½s own school the Christian part,
آ آ آ آ And bind the task assignï؟½d thee to thine heart:
آ آ آ آ Happy the man there seeking and there found:
آ آ آ آ Happy the nation where such men abound!
آ آ آ آ How shall a verse impress thee? by what name
آ آ آ آ Shall I adjure thee not to court thy shame?
آ آ آ آ By theirs whose bright example, unimpeachï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Directs thee to that eminence they reachï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Heroes and worthies of days past, thy sires?
آ آ آ آ Or his, who touchï؟½d their hearts with hallowï؟½d fires?
آ آ آ آ Their names, alas! in vain reproach an age,
آ آ آ آ Whom all the vanities they scornï؟½d engage;
آ آ آ آ And his, that seraphs tremble at, is hung
آ آ آ آ Disgracefully on every triflerï؟½s tongue,
آ آ آ آ Or serves the champion in forensic war,
آ آ آ آ To flourish and parade with at the bar.
آ آ آ آ Pleasure herself perhaps suggests a plea,
آ آ آ آ If interest move thee, to persuade eï؟½en thee;
آ آ آ آ By every charm that smiles upon her face,
آ آ آ آ By joys possessï؟½d, and joys still held in chase,
آ آ آ آ If dear society be worth a thought,
آ آ آ آ And if the feast of freedom cloy thee not,
آ آ آ آ Reflect that these, and all that seems thine own,
آ آ آ آ Held by the tenure of his will alone,
آ آ آ آ Like angels in the service of their Lord,
آ آ آ آ Remain with thee, or leave thee at his word;
آ آ آ آ That gratitude, and temperance in our use
آ آ آ آ Of what he gives, unsparing and profuse,
آ آ آ آ Secure the favour, and enhance the joy,
آ آ آ آ That thankless waste and wild abuse destroy.
آ آ آ آ But, above all, reflect how cheap soeï؟½er
آ آ آ آ Those rights, that millions envy thee, appear,
آ آ آ آ And though resolved to risk them, and swim down
آ آ آ آ The tide of pleasure, heedless of his frown,
آ آ آ آ That blessings truly sacred, and when given
آ آ آ آ Markï؟½d with the signature and stamp of Heaven,
آ آ آ آ The word of prophecy, those truths divine,
آ آ آ آ Which make that heaven, if thou desire it, thine
آ آ آ آ (Awful alternative! believe, beloved,
آ آ آ آ Thy glory, and thy shame if unimproved),
آ آ آ آ Are never long vouchsafed, if pushï؟½d aside
آ آ آ آ With cold disgust or philosophic pride;
آ آ آ آ And that, judicially withdrawn, disgrace,
آ آ آ آ Error, and darkness, occupy their place.
آ آ آ آ A world is up in arms, and thou, a spot
آ آ آ آ Not quickly found, if negligently sought,
آ آ آ آ Thy soul as ample as thy bounds are small,
آ آ آ آ Endurï؟½st the brunt, and darï؟½st defy them all;
آ آ آ آ And wilt thou join to this bold enterprise
آ آ آ آ A bolder still, a contest with the skies?
آ آ آ آ Remember, if He guard thee and secure,
آ آ آ آ Whoeï؟½er assails thee, thy success is sure;
آ آ آ آ But if He leave thee, though the skill and power
آ آ آ آ Of nations sworn to spoil thee and devour,
آ آ آ آ Were all collected in thy single arm,
آ آ آ آ And thou couldst laugh away the fear of harm,
آ آ آ آ That strength would fail, opposed against the push
آ آ آ آ And feeble onset of a pigmy rush.
آ آ آ آ Say not (and if the thought of such defence
آ آ آ آ Should spring within thy bosom, drive it thence)
آ آ آ آ What nation amongst all my foes is free
آ آ آ آ From crimes as base as any charged on me?
آ آ آ آ Their measure fillï؟½d, they too shall pay the debt,
آ آ آ آ Which God, though long forborne, will not forget.
آ آ آ آ But know that wrath divine, when most severe,
آ آ آ آ Makes justice still the guide of his career,
آ آ آ آ And will not punish, in one mingled crowd,
آ آ آ آ Them without light, and thee without a cloud.
آ آ آ آ Muse, hang this harp upon yon aged beech,
آ آ آ آ Still murmuring with the solemn truths I teach;
آ آ آ آ And, while at intervals a cold blast sings
آ آ آ آ Through the dry leaves, and pants upon the strings,
آ آ آ آ My soul shall sigh in secret, and lament
آ آ آ آ A nation scourged, yet tardy to repent.
آ آ آ آ I know the warning song is sung in vain;
آ آ آ آ That few will hear, and fewer heed the strain;
آ آ آ آ But if a sweeter voice, and one designï؟½d
آ آ آ آ A blessing to my country and mankind,
آ آ آ آ Reclaim the wandering thousands, and bring home
آ آ آ آ A flock so scatterï؟½d and so wont to roam,
آ آ آ آ Then place it once again between my knees;
آ آ آ آ The sound of truth will then be sure to please;
آ آ آ آ And truth alone, whereï؟½er my life be cast,
آ آ آ آ In scenes of plenty, or the pining waste,
آ آ آ آ Shall be my chosen theme, my glory to the last.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Cowper, titled "Expostulation.", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
